schools.summary: The purpose of the stones, a sign to prompt the children's question: 'that this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean ye by these stones?' — the teaching memorial — 'that this may be a sign among you, that when your children ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean ye by these stones?': the stones were set up as a sign that would prompt the children to ask, and so occasion the telling of God's works (the witnesses noting the significance — the memorial designed to provoke inquiry and so transmit the knowledge of God's wonders to the generations, the stones a standing sign, the children's question the occasion for teaching the deliverance, the lesson — that the works of God are to be remembered and taught to the children, the memorial keeping the testimony alive, the sign that calls forth the question, the fathers teaching the next generation, the truth that the great works of God are to be told to the children, 'we will not hide them from their children, shewing to the generation to come the praises of the LORD,' Psalm 78:4, the figure — the visible memorial that occasions the telling of redemption, like the passover, 'when your children shall say unto you, What mean ye by this service?... ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD's passover,' Exodus 12:26-27, the truth that the means of grace and the ordinances are appointed to provoke inquiry and teach the faith, the figure of the catechizing of the children, the sign that opens the way to instruction, the call to keep the memory of God's works alive in the generations, the warning that a people who forget God's works lose the faith, the figure of the memorial that teaches the next generation the works of God); schools.summary: That on that day the LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel: 'on that day the LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel; and they feared him, as they feared Moses, all the days of his life' — the divine establishment — 'on that day the LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel; schools.summary: Joshua setting up the twelve stones at Gilgal: 'and those twelve stones, which they took out of Jordan, did Joshua pitch in Gilgal' — Joshua set up the twelve stones at Gilgal as the memorial (the and those twelve stones, which they took out of Jordan, the stones from the river, the did Joshua pitch in Gilgal, set up at Gilgal, the figure, the memorial set up at the first camp in the land, the witness of the crossing, the truth that the stones were established at Gilgal, the abiding memorial, the figure of the memorial of God's wonders set up in the land, the witness for the generations, the stones at Gilgal, the testimony before all, the memorial established, vv.3); the stones taken from the river (the memorial) — the stones (from Jordan); 'did Joshua pitch in Gilgal' (the stones set up at Gilgal, the abiding memorial, the figure, the memorial of God's wonders in the land, the providence, the memorial, the meaning of the stones for the children to follow, vv.21-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And those twelve stones, which they took out of Jordan, did Joshua pitch in Gilgal
Joshua setting up the twelve stones at Gilgal
Joshua setting up the twelve stones at Gilgal, the first camp in the land, the abiding memorial
and witness of the crossing

schools.summary: The charge to teach the children the meaning of the stones: 'and he spake unto the children of Israel, saying, When your children shall ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean these stones?' — Joshua charged the people that when their children asked the meaning of the stones, they should tell them (the and he spake unto the children of Israel, saying, When your children shall ask their fathers in time to come, the children's question foreseen, the saying, What mean these stones?, the question, the figure, the memorial that prompts the children's question, the occasion for teaching, the truth that the stones were to provoke inquiry and the telling of God's works, as before, vv.6, the figure of the catechizing of the children, the question that opens instruction, the memorial that keeps the testimony alive, the children to be taught the wonder, vv.6); the children's question foreseen (the occasion for teaching) — the question (foreseen); 'What mean these stones?' (the children's inquiry the occasion for the telling, the catechizing, the figure, the memorial that opens instruction, the providence, the question, the answer of the dried Jordan to follow, vv.22-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he spake unto the children of Israel, saying, When your children shall ask their fathers in time to come, saying, What mean these stones?
the charge to teach the children the meaning of the stones
Joshua charging the people that the children's question should be the occasion for telling God's works
the memorial keeping the testimony alive

schools.summary: The answer, that Israel came over Jordan on dry land: 'then ye shall let your children know, saying, Israel came over this Jordan on dry land' — the children were to be told that Israel crossed the Jordan on dry land (the then ye shall let your children know, the children taught, the saying, Israel came over this Jordan on dry land, the crossing on dry land, the figure, the answer that recounts the wonder, Israel over on dry ground, the truth that the children were to know of the dry-land crossing, God's act remembered, the figure of the testimony handed down, the wonder told, the dry-land passage recounted, the deliverance taught to the next generation, vv.21); the children to be told the wonder (the testimony) — the teaching (let them know); 'Israel came over this Jordan on dry land' (the crossing on dry ground recounted, the wonder told, the figure, the testimony handed down, the providence, the answer, the drying of Jordan likened to the Red Sea to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then ye shall let your children know, saying, Israel came over this Jordan on dry land
the answer, that Israel came over Jordan on dry land
the children to be told that Israel crossed the Jordan on dry land
the wonder of God's deliverance taught to the next generation

schools.summary: The drying of Jordan likened to the Red Sea: 'for the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over, as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over' — the drying of Jordan was like the drying of the Red Sea, both works of the same God (the for the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over, the Jordan dried, the as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over, like the Red Sea, the figure, the two great deliverances joined, the Jordan and the Red Sea, the same God, the truth that the crossing of Jordan answered to the crossing of the Red Sea, the deliverance at the entrance as at the exit, the figure of the God who opens the way through the waters at the beginning and the end, 'he turned the sea into dry land,' Psalm 66:6, the parallel that joins the wilderness journey's start and close, the same wonder at the Red Sea and the Jordan, vv.22); the LORD drying up the Jordan (the wonder, Psalm 66:6) — the drying (the Jordan); 'as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up' (the Jordan like the Red Sea, the same God, the two deliverances joined, the figure, the God who opens the way at the beginning and the end, the providence, the parallel, the purpose that all the earth might know to follow, vv.24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: For the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over, as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over
the drying of Jordan likened to the Red Sea
the drying of Jordan like the drying of the Red Sea, the same God opening the way through the waters at the entrance as at the exit
the two great deliverances joined (Psalm 66:6)

schools.summary: The twofold purpose of the wonder, that all the earth might know God's hand and that Israel might fear him for ever: 'that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever' — the twofold end — 'that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever': the crossing was wrought that the nations might know God's power and that Israel might fear him for ever (the witnesses noting the significance — the great works of God designed both to make his power known to all the world and to teach his own people the fear of him, the wonder a testimony to the nations of God's might, and to Israel a ground of perpetual reverence, the lesson — that God's mighty acts serve his glory among the nations and the holiness of his people, the world to know him and the church to fear him, the twofold purpose, the display of power and the fruit of reverence, the truth that God works that his name be known in all the earth, 'that my name may be declared throughout all the earth,' Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17, the figure — the works of God that publish his glory to the world, 'declare his glory among the heathen, his wonders among all people,' Psalm 96:3, the truth that the end of all God's mighty acts is the knowledge of him and the fear of him, the world brought to acknowledge his hand and his people to reverence him, the figure of the missionary purpose of God's works, his power displayed that all might know, the call to the people to fear the LORD for ever as the fruit of his wonders, the warning to the nations that the hand of the LORD is mighty, the figure of the works of God designed to display his power to the world and to teach his people reverence); the wonder wrought that all the world might know the might of God's hand, his power published to the nations (the testimony to the world, Exodus 9:16) — the purpose (all the earth might know); 'that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever' (the wonder wrought also that Israel might fear God for ever, the reverence the fruit of his mighty acts, the end of his works the knowledge and fear of him, the perpetual reverence, Psalm 96:3) — the figure (the missionary purpose of God's works, his power displayed that all might know, the providence, the purpose, the close of the crossing narrative and the conquest to follow, ch.5) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What is the purpose of the wonder of the crossing?

sides.position: To display God's power to the world and to teach his people reverence - the great works of God designed both to make his might known to all the nations and to teach Israel the fear of him for ever, a testimony to the world and a ground of perpetual reverence (Exodus 9:16; Romans 9:17), a figure of the missionary purpose of God's works, his glory published among the heathen (Psalm 96:3)

themes: That all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever
the twofold purpose of the wonder, that all the earth might know God's hand and that Israel might fear him for ever (the crux of the works of God designed to display his power and teach reverence)
the great works of God designed both to make his power known to all the world and to teach his people perpetual reverence, a testimony to the nations and a ground of fear
a figure of the missionary purpose of God's works, his glory published among the heathen (Exodus 9:16; Psalm 96:3; Romans 9:17)
